# Ruby Learning Journal Walkthrough Document

## Setup

In the setup we will be installing Xcode, Homebrew, VScode, Rbenv, Ruby, PostgreSQL, Git, Bundler, Github and then creating a local directory to store our work.

Xcode \- Used to allow our macs to develop software applications  
Homebrew \- Used to help install software that we need to create the learn journal  
VScode \- A great free IDE with usefully extensions which we will be coding in  
Rbenv \- A command line tool used to install, manage and work with multiple Ruby versions   
Ruby \- Installed to let us code in the Ruby language  
PostgreSQL \- A database management system we use to store our application resources  
Git \- A DevOps tool used for source code management, this will be used to track the changes within our application files  
Bundler \- Tracks and install gems on the exact versions needed  
Github \- A popular code hosting platform used to remotely store our application, also implements version control in case we lose our work

### Installing Xcode

Used to allow our macs to develop software applications

- Open terminal application  
- Type the following command used to install xcode:

| xcode-select \--install |
| :---- |

- In the dialogue window, confirm that you want to install xcode tools.  
- Agree to the licence agreement and wait for the installation to complete

### Installing Homebrew

Used to help quickly install software that we need to create the learn journal

- Open terminal application  
- Type the following command used to install homebrew:

| /bin/bash \-c "$(curl \-fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Homebrew/install/HEAD/install.sh)" |
| :---- |

- Type in your admin password (note: you will not see your keystrokes in the terminal window)  
    
- If you see the following warning:![][image1]  
- Then run the following two commands:

| echo 'eval "$(/opt/homebrew/bin/brew shellenv)"' \>\> \~/.zprofileeval "$(/opt/homebrew/bin/brew shellenv)" |
| :---- |

- When the installation has successfully finished then run the following command:

| brew update |
| :---- |

- If you get a /usr/local must be writable error, then run the commands:

| sudo chown \-R $USER:admin /usr/local |
| :---- |

| brew update |
| :---- |

### Installing VScode

A great free IDE with usefully extensions which we will be coding in

- Go to the website: [https://code.visualstudio.com/](https://code.visualstudio.com/)  
- Click download VScode for mac  
- After installing VScode, open the application and use the integrated terminal instead of the terminal application

### Installing Rbenv

A command line tool used to install, manage and work with multiple ruby versions 

- Run the following command:

| brew install rbenv ruby-build |
| :---- |

- Type the following command into your terminal:

| open \~/.zshrc |
| :---- |

- Add the following code to the bottom of your zshrc file:

| eval "$(rbenv init \- zsh)" |
| :---- |

- Close your integrated terminal on VScode and reopen a new one to apply changes  
    
- If you get any problems, visit the rbenv github page: [https://github.com/rbenv/rbenv](https://github.com/rbenv/rbenv)

### Installing Ruby

Installed to let us code in the Ruby language

- Run the following command:

| rbenv install 3.1.2 |
| :---- |

- Then set your ruby version globally with the following command:

| rbenv global 3.1.2 |
| :---- |

- Check your ruby version is correctly set with the following command:

| ruby \-v |
| :---- |

- It is recommended to double check your ruby version before installing anything in the project. This ensures that the version installed is compatible with your application

### Installing PostgreSQL

A database management system we use to store our application resources

- Run the following command:

| brew install postgresql |
| :---- |

- Run the following command to start postgreSQL:

| brew services start postgresql |
| :---- |

- Run the following command to check postgreSQL is correctly running:

| brew services list |
| :---- |

- Create a superuser on postgreSQL with all permissions for our database later on with the following commands (make sure you change the \<\<username\>\> and \<\<password\>\>):

| sudo \-u postgres createuser \-s \-i \-d \-r \-l \-w \<\<username\>\> sudo \-u postgres psql \-c "ALTER ROLE \<\<username\>\> WITH PASSWORD '\<\<password\>\>';" |
| :---- |

- Launch postgreSQL by entering the following command:

| psql postgres |
| :---- |

- Check that your superuser has been correctly added with the following command:

| \\du |
| :---- |

- Exit postgreSQL with the following command:

| \\q  |
| :---- |

### Installing Git

A DevOps tool used for source code management, this will be used to track the changes within our application files

- Run the following command to install Git and Github:

| brew upgrade git || brew install gitbrew upgrade gh || brew install gh |
| :---- |

### Installing Bundler

Tracks and install gems and the exact versions needed

- Run the following command to install Bundler:

| brew bundle |
| :---- |

### Creating a Local Directory

- Run the following commands to create a new directory in your documents for the project:

| cd Documents/mkdir ruby\-grad-trainingcd ruby\-grad-training |
| :---- |

### Creating a Github Account (skip if you already have one)

A popular code hosting platform used to store our application, also implements version control in case we lose our work

#### Generating a SSH Key

This allows you to bypass authentication every time you save changes to your application.

- Follow the instruction in the following link to generate a new SSH key: [https://docs.github.com/en/authentication/connecting-to-github-with-ssh/generating-a-new-ssh-key-and-adding-it-to-the-ssh-agent](https://docs.github.com/en/authentication/connecting-to-github-with-ssh/generating-a-new-ssh-key-and-adding-it-to-the-ssh-agent)  
- Connect the new SSH key to your Github account by following the instructions in the following link:  
  [https://docs.github.com/en/authentication/connecting-to-github-with-ssh/adding-a-new-ssh-key-to-your-github-account](https://docs.github.com/en/authentication/connecting-to-github-with-ssh/adding-a-new-ssh-key-to-your-github-account)

#### Creating a Github Repository

This repository is where you will save your application on Github.

- Once you have connected your SSH key to your Github account, go to your Github repositories and create a new one by:  
- Clicking on the ‘New’ button  
- Selecting yourself as the owner of the repository  
- Inputting a name for your new repository  
- Setting your repository as private  
- Clicking on ‘Create repository’

### Generating a New Ruby Project

- Open VScode  
- Open the local directory we created  
- Ensure we are in the right directory \- your terminal should look like this:

![][image2]

- Double check your Ruby version \- it should be 3.1.2  
- Generate a new Ruby project with the following command (make sure you change the \<\<appname\>\>):

| rails new \<\<APPNAME\>\> \-d postgresql |
| :---- |

- Change your terminal into the correct directory by typing the following command:

| cd \<\<APPNAME\>\> |
| :---- |

- Create your database to store your tables with the following command:

| rails db:create |
| :---- |

### Commiting to Github

- Run the following commands:

| git initgit add \-Agit commit \-m "first commit"git branch \-M maingit remote add origin \<\<YOUR GIT REPO URL\>\>git push \-u origin main |
| :---- |

- Check that your first push has succeeded with the following command (no files pending):

| git status |
| :---- |

- Go to your Github repository and check that the files are all pushed correctly

I recommend regularly saving and sorting your work by pushing to github and using branches.

### Launching Rails Server

- Run the following command to launch your rails server:

| rails server |
| :---- |

- Open your browser (I recommend Chrome)  
- Type the following URL into your browser:

| localhost:3000 |
| :---- |

You should now see something similar to the following image:  
![][image3]

## Upgrading Your First Gem

In this section you will be upgrading the ‘pg’ Gem which is used for PostgreSQL

- Find and open the gemfile.rb file  
- Change the following code:

| gem "pg", "\~\> 1.1" |
| :---- |

- To the following code:

| gem "pg", "\~\> 1.4.5" |
| :---- |

- Run the following command to update your application with any changes in your Gemfile:

| bundle install |
| :---- |

## Displaying ‘Hello World’

- Find and open the routes.rb file  
- Create a new route by adding the following code:

| Rails.application.routes.draw do root 'application\#hello'end |
| :---- |

The above code tells the application to show a specific URL and display the ‘hello’ method from the ApplicationController.rb file.

- Find and open the application\_controller.rb file  
- Create a new method called ‘hello’ which renders the HTML ‘Hello World’

| class ApplicationController \< ActionController::Base   def hello       render html: "Hello World\!"   endend |
| :---- |

- Refresh your browser and you should see something similar to the following screenshot:

![][image4]

## Generating a Model and Migrating

A generated model will create a table in our schema. In the Ruby Learning Journal we require a simple string model that allows the user to input the name of a resource they want to save in their journal.

- Run the following command in your terminal (make sure you change \<\<Tablename\>\> and ensure it starts with a capital letter and is singular):

| rails generate model \<\<Tablename\>\> name:string |
| :---- |

You should see the following output in your Terminal:  
![][image5]  
The above output shows the creation of 4 new files (inline with create). The last 2 files we will ignore for now as they are used for testing purposes. The first file ending in ‘create\_entries.rb’ creates a migration that will add the new model to the database.   
![][image6]  
The second file ending in ‘entry.rb’ ’adds the model to our application. Note that yours may be different if you named your table name differently.![][image7]

- Migrate the generated model by running the following command:

| rails db:migrate |
| :---- |

The above command should also generate a new file called ‘schema.rb’. Take a look at this file as it shows the current state of the database. You should find your new model added to the file.

## Generating a Controller

- Enter the following command (make sure to change the \<\<ControllerName\>\> and ensure it uses camel case and is plural):

| rails generate controller \<\<ControllerName\>\> |
| :---- |

You should see the following output:  
![][image8]  
In the above screenshot we can see that the command generated 4 files again. The bottom two are used for testing purposes. The first created file is the ‘entries\_controller.rb’ file where we will be defining our methods just like the hello world example. The command also creates a new directory in app/views/\<\<ControllerName\>\> which will house our HTML to display on our URL.

- Find and open the generated controller  
- Create 3 new methods called \- Index, Create, New with the following code:

| class EntriesController \< ApplicationController   def index   end   def new   end   def create   endend |
| :---- |

## Creating and Displaying a Home Page

### Changing your route to the new controller

- Find and open your routes.rb file  
- Delete the ‘application\#hello’ root (Also delete the ‘hello’ method in your application\_controller.rb file)  
- Create routes for the 3 methods we have just created in the generated controller with the following code:

| Rails.application.routes.draw do root 'entries\#index' resources :entries, only: \[:create, :new\]end |
| :---- |

The above code will route your homepage to the index method within your new controller and using the resources call, it automatically creates routes for the other 2 methods we created in the controller.

### Adding Code in Your Controller to Display Your Resource

- Find and open your new controller  
- Add the following code within the index method to send all the data from the model/database to the index view:

| class EntriesController \< ApplicationController   def index       @entries \= Entry.all   end |
| :---- |

The above code will store all entries (which is my generated table) to an instance variable called ‘@entries’. This will be used later to display all the data in the view. You will not see anything displayed on your browser as we have not created a view yet.

- Add the following code within the new method to create a new instance of the Model’s Class that you generated:

| class EntriesController \< ApplicationController   def index       @entries \= Entry.all   end   def new       @entry \= Entry.new()   end |
| :---- |

The code in the ‘new’ method will use the built in method .new() to create a new instance of the Class Entry, which is the name of my generated model. This will let a user input a new entry to later save in the database.

- Add the following code within the create method and controller:

| class EntriesController \< ApplicationController   def index       @entries \= Entry.all   end   def new       @entry \= Entry.new()   end   def create       @entry \= Entry.new(entry\_params)       if @entry.save           redirect\_to root\_url       else           render :new       end   end   private   def entry\_params       params.require(:entry).permit(:name)   endend |
| :---- |

The code in the create method will attempt to save the user’s submitted entry with the permitted parameters called ‘entry\_params’. If the save is successful then the page will redirect to the root\_url which will show our index page. If the save was unsuccessful then the page will re-render the new page where the user attempted to create a new entry.  
The entry\_params method is private to ensure the method cannot be accessed by other places in the application which is great for security. It requires the specific parameter of entry (which is my model) and allows the user to upload a name to the database.

### Creating a View

- Go to the app/views/\<\<ControllerName\>\> directory  
- Create a new file called new.html.erb

![][image9]  
This ‘new’ view file will be used to display a form so that users can submit new entries within your learning journal. This view will be displayed on the URL: 

| localhost:3000/\<\<controllername\>\>/new |
| :---- |

### Adding Code to Your New Entry View

Now that we have created the view, we need to add HTML code that will display the form the user can interact with to add a new entry.

- Add the following code into your new.html.erb view file:

| \<h1\>New Entry\</h1\>\<%= form\_with(model: @entry) do |form| %\> \<div\>   \<%= form.label :name %\>   \<%= form.text\_field :name %\> \</div\> \<div\>   \<%= form.submit %\> \</div\>\<% end %\> |
| :---- |

- Go to the URL above to see the new form

The above code will display a form with a field labelled ‘name’ and a submit button. When the user submits this form, the information will be passed back to the controller \- into the ‘new’ and ‘create’ method. Where the new entry is then added to the database.

### Adding Code to Display Database Records

- Find and open the index.html.erb view file  
- Add the following code:

| \<h1\>All Entries\</h1\>\<div\> \<% @entries.each do |entry| %\>   \<%= entry.name %\> \<% end %\>\</div\> |
| :---- |

The above code loops through the @entries instance variable we declared in the ‘index’ method within the controller. This instance variable contains all the records added in our database. To display every single record, we loop through the variable with ‘.each do’ and print out the name of that record.

- Go to the root directory of your application and you should see something similar to this screenshot:

![][image10]

## Creating Navigation Bar and Buttons

### Creating a ‘Back to Home’ Button

- Find and open your HTML file that contains the new entry form  
- Add the following code to create a back button:

| \<h1\>New Entry\</h1\>\<%= form\_with(model: @entry) do |form| %\> \<div\>   \<%= form.label :name %\>   \<%= form.text\_field :name %\> \</div\> \<div\>   \<%= form.submit %\> \</div\>\<% end %\>\<br\>\<div\> \<%= link\_to "Back to home", root\_path %\>\</div\> |
| :---- |

The link\_to will redirect the page back to the root\_path which is our index.html.erb view. This allows the user to cancel creating a new entry and return to the page when they do not want to submit the form.

### Creating a Navigation Bar

We are now going to create a navigation bar. This bar will be consistently displayed at the top of your webpage on every page. To reduce duplicate code, we will first make a partial, which is a view file that can be called and rendered wherever we want that view to be shown. By using a partial for our navigation bar, we can just render the partial on our existing views instead of writing the code multiple times within each file.

- Create a new directory inside the view directory called ‘shared’  
- Create a new file within ‘shared’ called ‘\_navbar.html.erb’

Note that every partial begins with an underscore.

Here is what your view directory should look like:  
![][image11]

- Find and open the ‘\_navbar.html.erb’ file  
- Add the following code to create a simple navigation bar:

| \<div\> \<div\>   \<%= link\_to "Ruby learning journal", root\_path %\>   \<div class="navbar-link"\>     \<%= link\_to "New Entry", new\_entry\_path %\>   \</div\> \</div\>\</div\> |
| :---- |

The new\_entry\_path will link the user to the ‘new.html.erb’ view. Make sure you have the correct path by using the command rails routes in your terminal.

Now that the navbar is ready, we need to render the partial on top of every page to consistently show the new navbar.

- Find and open the ‘application.html.erb’ file  
- Add the following code to display the navbar on all pages:

| \<\!DOCTYPE html\>\<html\> \<head\>   \<title\>RubyLearnJounral\</title\>   \<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width,initial-scale=1"\>   \<%= csrf\_meta\_tags %\>   \<%= csp\_meta\_tag %\>   \<%= stylesheet\_link\_tag "application", "data-turbo-track": "reload" %\>   \<%= javascript\_importmap\_tags %\> \</head\> \<body\>   \<%= render '/shared/navbar' %\>   \<%= yield %\> \</body\>\</html\> |
| :---- |

- Refresh your browser and you should see something similar to this screenshot:

![][image12]

## Adding a Second Resource 

Now that you have successfully created and displayed the name of your entry, we would like you to create a second field that displays a link.

To do this, you will need to run a migration that will add a new column to your database table. This column will show up in your ‘schema.rb’ file.

- Alter the following command to add a new field to your table:

| rails generate migration add\_fieldname\_to\_tablename fieldname:string  |
| :---- |

Note the above command is in snake case which uses underscores to separate words. You can also use CamelCase which uses capitals to separate words:

| rails generate migration AddFieldnameToTablename fieldname:string |
| :---- |

Hint: for the above challenge, you will need to change the field name to something to do with your new resource and the table name to match the table your entries are currently saved in.

- Enter the altered command into your terminal

The generated migration should look something like this:

| class AddLinkToEntries \< ActiveRecord::Migration\[7.0\] def change   add\_column :entries, :link, :string endend |
| :---- |

And your ‘schema.rb’ file should look something like this after you have migrated:

| ActiveRecord::Schema\[7.0\].define(version: 2023\_02\_16\_162827) do \# These are extensions that must be enabled in order to support this database enable\_extension "plpgsql" create\_table "entries", force: :cascade do |t|   t.string "name"   t.datetime "created\_at", null: false   t.datetime "updated\_at", null: false   t.string "link" endend |
| :---- |

Now that you have added the new ‘link’ string to your database, you will now have to reproduce or edit code we have written for the ‘name’ string to allow the ‘link’ string to have the same functionality.  
As a hint, the link should:

- Be displayed on the home page next to the name  
  - Update your index view file  
- Be a field that can be submitted just like name in your ‘new’ form  
  - Update your new file  
  - Permit the link within your create method in the Controller

##  ,Creating a Delete Button for Your Entries

- Find and open your Controller  
- Add the following code to create a destroy method:

|    def destroy       Entry.find(params\[:id\]).destroy       redirect\_to root\_url   end |
| :---- |

The above code will use the .find() built-in Ruby method to find the record that we want to delete. The params\[:id\] specifies the record we want to delete by matching the ID on the web page to the ID in the database. The method .destroy simply deletes that record.

- Add the destroy method to your routes.rb file (similarly to the methods)  
- Add the following code to your index view to display the delete button:

| \<h1\>All Entries\</h1\>\<div\> \<% @entries.each do |entry| %\>   \<%= entry.name %\>   \<%= entry.link %\>   \<%= button\_to "Delete", entry, method: :delete, data: { confirm: "Are you sure you want to delete this Project ?"} %\> \<% end %\>\</div\> |
| :---- |

Your website should look something similar to this screenshot:  
![][image13]  
Congratulations if you have made it this far, the minimum viable product is now functionally finished. All that is left is styling.

## Styling Using Bootstrap

Bootstrap is the styling framework I will be using and teaching as it allows users to quickly and easily make our application look presentable and responsive. If you wish to use other styling frameworks or CSS/SCSS, feel free to.

- Find and open your gemfile  
- Enable the sassc-rails gem by adding it or uncommenting it if it's already there.  
- Find the rails Bootstrap Gem online  
- Add the rails Bootstrap gem to your gemfile  
- Run the following command to install the new gems:

| bundle install |
| :---- |

- Import Bootstrap into your application.css file using the following code:

| @import "bootstrap"; |
| :---- |

- Change the application.css file to an application.scss file which is required for bootstrap to work by entering the following command in your terminal:

| *mv app/assets/stylesheets/application.css app/assets/stylesheets/application.scss* |
| :---- |

- Restart your server

Your application should now look something like the following screenshots:  
![][image14]![][image15]  
Now that bootstrap is imported and working, it is up to you to style your application.  
You can use the bootstrap documentation([https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/getting-started/introduction/](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/getting-started/introduction/)) to research the different classes that you want to apply in your html to make your application look better or you can do your own design using css/sscs or other frameworks.

You will need to edit the HTML in your view files to get some styling options to work.

Here are some screenshots of my application after some simple Bootstrap components:  
![][image16]![][image17]

## Additional Features

At this point, congratulations \- you have finished the base mvp learning journal. We recommend you continue to add extra functionality/features and add your own personal touch to make the application uniquely yours.

Additional feature ideas:

1. Additional information on entries  
2. Display the entries in a better format \- hint: tables, cards or other (check Bootstrap examples)  
3. Form and value validation  
4. Functionality to edit an existing entry  
5. Search bar for entries \- hint: look for a gem  
6. Ability to upload image to an entry \-  hint: look for a gem  
7. Add tags to filter/sort entries together  
8. Deploy your application \- hint: look for free servers to deploy your app, I recommend github pages

The above are some ideas that range from low to high difficulty. But please do implement other ideas to show off your application.
